steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The conclusion states that distinguishing spontaneous from volitional laughter is universal across cultures. A strengthening finding must provide consistent, cross-cultural evidence that listeners everywhere can make this distinction.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Listeners from both Japan and Turkey tend to be able to determine how well two people know each other based on recordings of their conversation.': This is about inferring social familiarity from conversation, not about distinguishing laugh types. Irrelevant. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Spontaneous laughter typically has a recognizably higher pitch than volitional laughter, regardless of what society the speakers in a conversation are from.': Identifies a measurable acoustic feature (higher pitch) that holds across all societies. This gives listeners everywhere an objective, consistent cue — directly supporting universality. ✓
  • Choice C — 'There is some evidence of correlation between the average size of communities in a society and judgments of spontaneous and volitional laughter by listeners in that society.': Suggests the ability varies with community size, which undermines rather than supports universality. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Acoustic features of spontaneous laughter, such as pitch and intensity, have measurable variations both within and across societies.': Emphasizes variation, which cuts against the idea of a consistent, universal distinguishing feature. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
